title: To require congressional approval before the sale, disposal, declaration of excess or surplus, transfer, or conveyance of Federal property with historical significance, and for other purposes.
plain_summary: This bill says that the President and government agencies cannot sell or give away federal buildings or land that are historically important without asking Congress first. They have to tell Congress about their plans and wait for Congress to vote yes before they can do anything with the property. This rule makes sure that Congress gets to decide what happens to government-owned places that are important to American history. The goal is to protect historical federal property from being sold or transferred without lawmakers having a say in the decision.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Economic Development, Public Buildings, and Emergency Management.

sections.title: Section 1  Congressional Approval of Sale of Federal Property with Historical Significance
sections.text: The President or any federal agency leader cannot sell, get rid of, declare as extra, transfer, or give away any historic federal property without following these steps. First, they must send Congress a written notice saying they want to sell, get rid of, declare as extra, transfer, or give away the historic property. Second, Congress must pass a joint resolution saying they approve the action described in that notice. A covered building means any land, building, structure, monument, or site that is owned by the United States and is listed or has ever been listed on the National Register of Historic Places. The National Register of Historic Places is the official list of places in America that are important to our history. A specified official means the President, the head of any federal agency, and any other federal official.
